title: Rapid Prototyping with AI
description: Learn to use Claude to accelerate your prototyping workflow — from generating UI copy to creating interaction flows to building functional prototypes.
learningObjectives: Use Claude to generate diverse design concepts, variations, and alternatives that expand your creative exploration beyond initial ideas
Generate production-quality UI copy and microcopy with Claude, including error messages, onboarding text, and contextual help
Leverage Claude for design critiques that identify usability issues, accessibility gaps, and interaction problems in your prototypes

sections.title: AI-Assisted Design Ideation
sections.content:
Design ideation is the phase where you generate as many concepts, approaches, and variations as possible before narrowing down. Claude is exceptionally good at this because it can produce volume without creative fatigue — and more importantly, it can explore directions that might not occur to you because of your own design biases.

**Generating Concept Variations**

The biggest risk in design ideation isn't bad ideas — it's too few ideas. Designers often converge on a solution too quickly because exploring alternatives is time-consuming. Claude eliminates that bottleneck.

• **Divergent exploration** — Give Claude your design brief and ask for 8-10 fundamentally different approaches to solving the problem. Not variations of one idea — genuinely different concepts. "I'm designing a feature that helps users organize their bookmarks. Give me 10 different conceptual approaches, ranging from minimal to radical." The goal is to map the solution space before committing to a direction.
• **Constraint-based variations** — Once you have a concept, use Claude to explore variations by changing one constraint at a time. "How would this design change if the user is on mobile? If they're a first-time user? If they have 1,000 bookmarks instead of 50? If they're visually impaired?" Each constraint shift reveals different design priorities.
• **Cross-domain inspiration** — Ask Claude to draw analogies from other domains. "How do email clients handle message organization? How do music apps handle playlist curation? How do physical filing systems work?" Borrowing patterns from adjacent domains often sparks creative solutions.

**Structured Brainstorming with Claude**

Claude works best in ideation when you give it structured brainstorming frameworks rather than open-ended requests:

• **SCAMPER method** — Ask Claude to apply each SCAMPER lens to your design: Substitute (what elements could be replaced?), Combine (what features could merge?), Adapt (what patterns from elsewhere could apply?), Modify (what could be larger, smaller, or rearranged?), Put to another use (how else could this feature serve users?), Eliminate (what could be removed entirely?), Reverse (what if the interaction flow were inverted?).
• **"How Might We" generation** — Give Claude your research insights and ask it to generate 10-15 "How Might We" questions. Then pick the most promising ones and ask Claude to brainstorm solutions for each. This bridges research and ideation cleanly.
• **Worst-case ideation** — Ask Claude to generate deliberately terrible solutions to your design problem. Then ask it to identify what makes each solution terrible. Often, the inverse of a terrible idea is an interesting one that you wouldn't have considered otherwise.

**Using AI Ideation Effectively**

Claude generates volume, but volume without curation is noise:

• **Generate, then curate, then refine.** Use Claude for the divergent phase (generating lots of ideas). Use your design judgment for the convergent phase (selecting the best ones). Then use Claude again for the refinement phase (developing the selected ideas further).
• **Push past the obvious.** Claude's first suggestions will often be conventional. The interesting ideas usually appear after you say "These are too predictable. Give me 5 more ideas that are unconventional, surprising, or even slightly uncomfortable." Claude responds well to creative provocation.
• **Combine the best elements.** After generating many concepts, ask Claude to combine the strongest elements from different concepts into hybrid approaches. "Take the simplicity of concept 3, the visual metaphor from concept 7, and the interaction model from concept 1. How would those combine?"

sections.title: Generating UI Copy and Microcopy
sections.content:
UI copy is the text that users read inside your product — button labels, form instructions, error messages, success confirmations, onboarding text, tooltips, and empty states. Despite being small in size, microcopy has an outsized impact on usability. Claude can generate, iterate, and refine UI copy much faster than writing it from scratch, and it's remarkably good at matching tone and voice guidelines.

**Error Messages That Actually Help**

Error messages are where most products fail users most visibly. Claude can generate error copy that follows best practices:

• **Structure: What happened + Why + What to do** — Ask Claude to generate error messages using this three-part structure. Instead of "Invalid input," you get "This email address doesn't look right. Make sure it includes an @ symbol and a domain (like name@company.com)." Specify the technical error and let Claude humanize it.
• **Tone calibration** — Give Claude your brand voice guidelines and ask it to write the same error message in three tones: formal, friendly, and minimal. Choose the version that matches your product's personality. "Hmm, that password is too short" suits a casual app. "Password must be at least 8 characters" suits a banking platform.
• **Generate in batches** — List all your error states (form validation, API errors, permission errors, timeout errors) and ask Claude to write copy for all of them at once. This ensures consistency across the entire error experience — tone, structure, and level of detail should be uniform.

**Onboarding and Instructional Copy**

Onboarding copy guides new users through their first experience and is often the most underinvested copy in a product:

• **Progressive disclosure copy** — Ask Claude to write copy for a multi-step onboarding flow. Each step should: explain what's happening, tell the user what to do, and preview what comes next. "Step 2 of 4: Choose your team. Add the people you work with most — you can always add more later. Next, we'll help you set up your first project."
• **Empty state messaging** — Empty states (no data yet, no items created, no results found) are opportunities to educate and motivate. Ask Claude to generate empty state copy that tells users what they'll see when data exists and what action to take now. "No projects yet. Create your first project to start organizing your team's work."
• **Tooltip and contextual help** — For complex features, ask Claude to write short tooltips (under 20 words) that explain what a feature does without jargon. Then write longer help text (2-3 sentences) for users who need more detail. Layer the help to match the user's level of need.

**Generating Variations for Testing**

One of Claude's biggest advantages in copywriting is its ability to generate many variations quickly:

• **A/B test candidates** — Ask Claude to generate 3-5 variations of a CTA button, headline, or value proposition. Specify the dimension you want to vary: tone (urgent vs. calm), length (short vs. descriptive), or framing (benefit-focused vs. action-focused). Use these as starting points for A/B testing.
• **Localization-ready copy** — When writing copy that will be translated, ask Claude to flag idioms, cultural references, or wordplay that might not translate well. Ask for simpler alternatives that convey the same meaning across cultures.
• **Accessibility-first copy** — Ask Claude to review your copy for screen reader compatibility. Labels should make sense when read aloud and out of visual context. "Click here" means nothing to a screen reader. "Download your invoice" is clear in any context.

sections.title: Creating Interaction Flows and User Stories
sections.content:
Interaction flows document how users move through your product — the screens they see, the actions they take, and the decisions they make. User stories capture what users need to accomplish and why. Both are essential prototyping artifacts, and Claude accelerates their creation significantly.

**Generating User Stories**

User stories translate user needs into actionable development requirements:

• **The standard format** — Ask Claude to generate user stories in the classic format: "As a [user type], I want to [action] so that [benefit]." Provide the feature description and user research context. Claude generates stories that cover the primary use case plus edge cases you might not have considered.
• **Acceptance criteria** — For each user story, ask Claude to generate 3-5 acceptance criteria — specific, testable conditions that must be true for the story to be considered complete. "Given the user is on the dashboard, when they click 'Export,' then a CSV file downloads containing all visible data with the current date in the filename."
• **Edge case stories** — After generating the main user stories, ask Claude: "What user stories are missing? What happens when the data is empty, when the user lacks permissions, when the network is slow, when the user is on a mobile device?" These edge case stories prevent surprises during implementation.

**Mapping Interaction Flows**

Interaction flows are step-by-step descriptions of how users move through a feature:

• **Happy path first** — Ask Claude to describe the ideal flow: user enters the feature, takes the expected actions, and achieves their goal. Be specific about each screen, interaction, and transition. "User clicks 'New Project' > Modal appears with project name field and template selector > User types name and selects template > User clicks 'Create' > Dashboard updates with new project card > Success toast appears."
• **Branch points** — At each step in the happy path, ask Claude to identify decision points and alternative paths. "What if the user changes their mind? What if the template they want isn't available? What if they navigate away mid-creation?" Each branch creates a fork in the flow that needs a design solution.
• **Error paths** — For each interaction, ask Claude to identify what can go wrong and describe the error flow. "What if the project name already exists? What if the API call to create the project fails? What if the user's session expires during creation?" Error paths are often the majority of the total flow complexity.

**Using Claude for Flow Validation**

Before building prototypes, validate your flows with Claude:

• **Heuristic evaluation** — Describe a flow to Claude and ask it to evaluate against Nielsen's usability heuristics. "Does this flow provide adequate feedback? Is the system status visible? Can users easily undo their actions?" Claude identifies heuristic violations you might have overlooked.
• **Cognitive walkthrough** — Ask Claude to role-play as a first-time user encountering the flow. "Walk through this flow as someone who has never used this product. At each step, describe: What are you trying to do? Can you see how to do it? After you do it, do you understand what happened?" This reveals assumptions your design makes about user knowledge.
• **Edge case stress test** — Ask Claude to find the weakest point in your flow — the step where the most users would get confused, drop off, or make errors. Then iterate on that specific step.

sections.title: Using Claude for Design Critiques
sections.content:
Design critique is the process of systematically evaluating a design against criteria like usability, accessibility, consistency, and user needs. Claude can serve as an always-available, never-biased critique partner — it doesn't have organizational politics, doesn't avoid conflict, and doesn't run out of energy.

**Structured Critique Frameworks**

The quality of Claude's critique depends entirely on the framework you give it:

• **Heuristic evaluation** — Describe your design to Claude and ask it to evaluate against established heuristics. Jakob Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics are the most common starting point. For each heuristic, ask Claude to identify violations and suggest improvements. Be specific in your description — include layout, labels, interactions, and error states.
• **Accessibility audit** — Ask Claude to evaluate your design for WCAG compliance. Describe your color choices, font sizes, interactive elements, and navigation patterns. Claude can identify issues like insufficient contrast ratios, missing alt text descriptions, keyboard navigation gaps, and focus management problems.
• **Consistency check** — Describe multiple screens or components from your design and ask Claude to identify inconsistencies in terminology, spacing, button styles, or interaction patterns. "On the settings page, the save action is a blue button labeled 'Save Changes.' On the profile page, it's a green button labeled 'Update.' Are there other inconsistencies like this?"

**Getting Useful Critiques**

Claude defaults to polite, balanced feedback. Push it for more actionable critique:

• **Ask for the top 3 problems** — Instead of asking for general feedback, ask Claude to identify the three most significant usability issues in your design, ranked by severity. This forces prioritization and prevents a laundry list of minor issues.
• **Request specific improvement suggestions** — Don't accept "the navigation could be improved." Ask Claude to describe exactly what it would change and why. "Replace the hamburger menu with a persistent bottom navigation bar showing the four primary sections. Rationale: hamburger menus hide primary navigation behind a tap, increasing the effort to switch between sections."
• **Use multiple critique lenses** — Run the same design through several critique frameworks: usability, accessibility, emotional design, information architecture, and performance. Each lens reveals different issues. A design that passes usability heuristics might fail an accessibility audit.

**Iterative Critique Cycles**

The most productive use of Claude for critique is iterative refinement:

• **Describe → Critique → Revise → Re-critique** — Start by describing your design. Get Claude's critique. Describe your revised design (addressing the top issues). Get another round of critique. Repeat until the critique surfaces only minor issues.
• **Track critique patterns** — If Claude consistently flags the same type of issue across multiple designs (e.g., insufficient feedback after user actions), you've identified a systematic gap in your design practice that's worth addressing at the process level.
• **Compare alternatives** — Describe two or three design alternatives to Claude and ask it to evaluate the tradeoffs of each. "Option A uses a modal for data entry. Option B uses an inline expansion. Option C navigates to a new page. Compare these three approaches for a user who needs to enter data for 15 items." This is faster than building and testing all three.

sections.title: Generate and Refine UI Copy with Claude
sections.content: Practice using Claude to generate production-quality UI copy across different contexts and interaction states.

exercise.prompt:
You're designing a feature that lets users invite team members to a shared workspace. The feature includes an invitation form, email sending, and acceptance flow.

Use Claude as your writing partner to generate the complete copy for this feature:

1. **Invitation form copy** — Write all the UI text for the invite form: the header, the input field labels and placeholders, the helper text, the role selector options (Admin, Editor, Viewer) with brief descriptions of each role, and the send button.

2. **Confirmation and status messages** — Write the copy for: (a) invite sent successfully, (b) invite sent but the email bounced, (c) the invited person already has an account, (d) the user has reached their plan's seat limit.

3. **Invitation email** — Write the subject line and body of the invitation email. It should: identify who sent the invitation, what workspace they're being invited to, what role they'll have, and include a clear call-to-action. Keep it concise — under 100 words for the body.

4. **Acceptance flow** — Write the copy for three screens: (a) landing page when the invitee clicks the email link (what they see before accepting), (b) success state after accepting, (c) expired invitation state with a path forward.

5. **Edge case copy** — Write copy for: (a) a user tries to invite themselves, (b) a user tries to invite someone who was previously removed from the workspace, (c) the inviter's permissions change between sending and the invitee accepting.

For all copy, use a tone that is professional but warm — like a well-run startup, not a corporate enterprise or a casual social app.
hints: Role descriptions should be concise enough to fit in a dropdown but clear enough to prevent users from choosing the wrong role — focus on what each role CAN do rather than what it can't
The bounced email message should help the user solve the problem — suggest checking the email address for typos rather than just reporting the failure
For the invitation email, remember that the recipient may not know what the product is — include enough context for them to understand the invitation without being familiar with the platform
Edge case copy is where your tone is tested most — the 'inviting yourself' message should feel lighthearted, while the 'previously removed' message should be handled carefully and neutrally
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) Complete invitation form copy that includes all specified elements — no missing labels or placeholders. Role descriptions should be concise (under 15 words each) but clear about permissions. (2) Four distinct status messages that follow the 'what happened + what to do next' pattern. The seat limit message should include a path to upgrade, not just report the problem. (3) An invitation email under 100 words that includes all required elements (sender, workspace, role, CTA) and works for recipients who may not know the product. (4) Three acceptance flow screens with appropriate copy for each state — the expired invitation must provide a clear path forward (not just 'sorry'). (5) Three edge case messages with appropriate tone — the self-invite should be lighthearted, and the removed-user message should be neutral and non-judgmental. Deduct points for generic copy that doesn't match the specified tone, for missing elements in any section, or for error messages that only report problems without suggesting solutions.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Using Claude for rapid prototyping accelerates every phase of the design process without replacing your judgment:

• **Use Claude to expand the solution space.** The biggest ideation risk is converging too early. Claude generates volume without fatigue — use it to explore 10 concepts before committing to one. Push past the obvious first suggestions to find genuinely surprising approaches.

• **UI copy is a design material.** Every label, error message, and tooltip shapes the user experience. Claude generates production-quality copy faster than writing from scratch, but always review for tone consistency, brand alignment, and contextual accuracy.

• **Document flows before building.** User stories, interaction flows, and edge case maps are cheaper than prototypes. Use Claude to identify gaps, branch points, and error states before you open your design tool.

• **Make critique a habit, not an event.** Claude is an always-available, never-judgmental critique partner. Run your designs through structured critique frameworks early and often — the cost of finding problems increases dramatically as you move from concept to prototype to code.
